title: Verizon Gets Booted From the Dow Jones Industrial Average. Is the Stock in Trouble?
author: David Jagielski, Cpa
description: Verizon was removed from the Dow Jones Industrial Average on June 29, 2026, to make room for Alphabet and increase the index's exposure to artificial intelligence and technology. While the stock declined about 8% in the past month following the announcement, the article argues this removal doesn't indicate fundamental business problems. With strong fundamentals, a modest valuation (11x earnings), and a high dividend yield of 6.4%, Verizon could present a buying opportunity for dividend investors despite near-term selling pressure.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: While the stock faced near-term selling pressure and an 8% decline following Dow removal, the article emphasizes strong fundamentals, safe dividend, modest valuation, and positions it as a potential buying opportunity. The removal itself is not indicative of business troubles, making the overall outlook balanced rather than negative.

Alphabet was added to the Dow Jones Industrial Average as a replacement for Verizon, reflecting the index's desire to increase exposure to artificial intelligence and technology, positioning it favorably.

title: SpaceX Stock Faces $4.3B Index Inflow and August Lock-Up Risk
author: Jeffrey Neal Johnson
description: SpaceX will enter the Nasdaq-100 index on July 7, triggering an estimated $4.3 billion in forced institutional buying. While this creates a near-term liquidity catalyst, the stock faces significant headwinds: a $2.1 trillion valuation with a P/S ratio of 108x, current annual losses of $4.9 billion, and upcoming lock-up expiration on August 6 that could flood the market with insider shares. Analysts warn of potential multiple compression once restricted shares become available.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Mixed outlook with near-term positive catalysts (index inclusion, $4.3B inflow) offset by significant fundamental concerns (108x P/S ratio, $4.9B annual losses, stretched $2.1T valuation). August lock-up expiration presents downside risk.

Potential terrestrial backhaul partnership with Starlink Mobile positions the company to benefit from satellite broadband scaling without bearing infrastructure costs, creating new revenue opportunities.
Starlink Mobile partnership with Charter Communications threatens Verizon's regional monopoly by enabling direct-to-consumer wireless competition without traditional infrastructure requirements.
Similar to Verizon, AT&T faces competitive pressure from Starlink Mobile's ability to challenge incumbent wireless carriers through satellite-based connectivity.

title: Alphabet Just Joined the Dow Jones Industrial Average. 3 Dow Dividend Stocks to Buy Now.
author: Patrick Sanders
description: Alphabet joined the Dow Jones Industrial Average on June 29, replacing Verizon. While Alphabet is a strong company with growing cloud and AI segments, its low dividend yield (0.3%) makes it less suitable for income investors. The article recommends three alternative Dow dividend stocks: Cisco Systems, Coca-Cola, and Amgen, all offering stronger dividend yields and solid year-to-date performance.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Strong advertising business, fast-growing cloud segment with 63% revenue growth, and significant AI infrastructure investments. However, limited by low dividend yield (0.3%).

Stock up 47% year-to-date, strong Q3 earnings with 12% revenue growth and 35% net income growth. AI products driving renewed investor attention. Dividend yield of 1.5% with 14 consecutive years of increases.
Stock up 18.2% year-to-date, Q1 revenue up 12% with organic growth of 10%. Strong dividend yield of 2.6% with 65 consecutive years of dividend increases (Dividend King status).

Stock up nearly 10% year-to-date, Q1 revenue up 6% with 16 products posting double-digit sales growth. Anti-obesity drug MariTide shows promise. Dividend yield of 2.8% with 15 consecutive years of increases.

title: Alphabet Just Replaced Verizon in the Dow. Could Nike Be the Next Dow Stock to Be Deleted?
author: Daniel Foelber
description: Alphabet has replaced Verizon in the Dow Jones Industrial Average following Honeywell's aerospace spinoff. Nike is now the lowest-priced Dow stock at around $41.46, making it vulnerable to removal. The company's turnaround has taken longer than expected due to failed direct-to-consumer strategy shifts, weak consumer spending, and inflationary pressures. While Nike remains a strong brand with a 24-year dividend history, Meta Platforms is suggested as a potential replacement if Nike is removed from the index.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Just added to the Dow Jones Industrial Average, replacing Verizon, indicating strong market position and index recognition.

title: Why Amazon, Alphabet, and Other Tech Stocks Popped Today
author: Joe Tenebruso
description: Amazon and Alphabet stocks climbed on Monday following reports of AWS raising GPU access prices by up to 20%, signaling strong cloud computing demand. AWS price increases help Amazon offset AI infrastructure costs while preserving margins. Google Cloud's faster growth suggests similar demand tailwinds. Alphabet also gained from its inclusion in the Dow Jones Industrial Average, replacing Verizon.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: AWS price increases of up to 20% indicate strong demand for cloud services and AI infrastructure. Price hikes help preserve profit margins despite rising chip costs, and AWS is a high-margin business critical to earnings growth.

Google Cloud is growing faster than AWS, suggesting it benefits from similar strong cloud demand. Additionally, Alphabet's inclusion in the Dow Jones Industrial Average likely spurred buying pressure on the stock.
